step.title: Step 33
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 46, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 45 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the N(W) token moved RIGHT 6 times from cols 12-14 to cols 30-32. But wait, it was at rows 21-23 (from the segmentation), and the wall starts at row 21. So the N(W) token is now at rows 21-23, cols 30-32, which is adjacent to the wall at cols 33-38.
But the wall is at rows 21-41, so the N(W) token at rows 21-23 is at the same vertical level as the top of the wall. This means moving RIGHT further would hit the wall.
Wait, but earlier I thought the N(W) token was at rows 18-20 (above the wall). Let me check the segmentation again.
The segmentation shows N(W) at boundary=[[21, 12], [21, 14], [23, 14], [23, 12]], which means rows 21-23, cols 12-14. But I moved it UP 6 times from rows 36-38:
- 36-38 → 33-35 → 30-32 → 27-29 → 24-26 → 21-23 → 18-20
That's 6 UP actions, which should put it at rows 18-20. But the segmentation shows rows 21-23. So maybe the UP actions didn't all execute, or the movement is different than I thought.
Wait, looking at the result, it says 6 UP actions were executed. But the N(W) token is at rows 21-23, not 18-20. Let me recalculate:
- Start: rows 36-38
- After 1 UP: 33-35
- After 2 UP: 30-32
- After 3 UP: 27-29
- After 4 UP: 24-26
- After 5 UP: 21-23
- After 6 UP: 18-20
So after 6 UP actions, it should be at rows 18-20. But the segmentation shows rows 21-23. This is confusing.
Wait, maybe the N(W) token was blocked by something. Let me check if there's an obstacle at rows 18-20, cols 12-14.
Actually, looking at the image, the N(W) token appears to be at the top edge of the left container area, not above it. The left container area (light gray background) seems to extend from rows 21-41. So maybe the N(W) token can't move above row 21 because there's no background there.
Actually, wait. The segmentation shows the N(W) token at rows 21-23, which is at the top of the light gray background area. So the N(W) token is constrained to stay within the light gray background area.
But the wall is at rows 21-41, cols 33-38. The N(W) token is at rows 21-23, cols 30-32 (after moving RIGHT 6 times). To get past the wall, I need to move the N(W) token to rows above 21, but it seems like it can't go above row 21.
Hmm, but the wall starts at row 21, and the N(W) token is at rows 21-23. So the N(W) token is at the same vertical level as the top of the wall. If I try to move RIGHT, it would hit the wall.
Wait, but the right container is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. The right side of the wall (cols 39-53) has a light gray background. So maybe I can move the N(W) token around the wall by going below it instead of above it.
The wall ends at row 41. The N(W) token is at rows 21-23. If I move it DOWN to rows below 41, I can then move it RIGHT past the wall.
But wait, the N(W) token is currently at rows 21-23, cols 30-32. If I move it RIGHT, it would hit the wall at cols 33-38. So I need to first move it DOWN to rows below 41, then RIGHT.
Actually, let me reconsider. The N(W) token is at rows 21-23, which is at the top of the light gray area. The wall is at rows 21-41. So the N(W) token is at the same vertical level as the top of the wall.
If I move the N(W) token DOWN, it goes to rows 24-26, 27-29, 30-32, etc. Eventually, it reaches rows 42-44, which is below the wall. Then I can move it RIGHT past the wall.
But this is getting complicated. Let me think about a simpler approach.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image again, the N(W) token is at the top of the left container area, near the gap between the left and right areas. The wall is purple and is between the left and right areas. The N(W) token is to the left of the wall.
Looking at the image, I see:
- N(W) at top of left area (green square)
- N(c) near the wall (green-bordered square with dark center)
- Left container with N(c) inside (charcoal-bordered square with white center)
Wait, there are THREE N tokens now? That doesn't make sense. Let me check the segmentation again.
Actually, looking at the segmentation output, there are only 2 N tokens:
1. N(W) at rows 21-23, cols 12-14 (but after 6 RIGHT actions, it should be at cols 30-32)
2. N(c) at rows 30-32, cols 30-32
Wait, the segmentation was printed BEFORE the 6 RIGHT actions. Let me check the current state after the RIGHT actions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after RIGHT actions
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== Current N tokens ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")
        if n['children']:
            child = next(x for x in seg if x['id'] == n['children'][0])
            print(f"    center: {child['color']}")

# The N(W) token was at rows 21-23, cols 12-14 before RIGHT actions
# After 6 RIGHT actions (each moves 3 cols): 12→15→18→21→24→27→30
# So N(W) should now be at rows 21-23, cols 30-32
# But the wall is at cols 33-38, rows 21-41
# So N(W) is adjacent to the wall at cols 33

# To get past the wall, I need to move N(W) DOWN below the wall (rows > 41)
# Then RIGHT past the wall
# Then UP to the right container

# But first, let me check if N(W) can move RIGHT into the wall
# If it's blocked, I need to go around

# Let me try moving DOWN to get below the wall
# Current: rows 21-23
# Wall ends at row 41
# Need to move DOWN: 21→24→27→30→33→36→39→42
# That's 7 DOWN actions to get to rows 42-44

# But wait, there's also N(c) at rows 30-32, cols 30-32
# If I move N(W) DOWN, it might collide with N(c)
# N(W) is at cols 30-32, N(c) is at cols 30-32
# So they're in the same column range!
# Moving N(W) DOWN would eventually reach rows 30-32, where N(c) is

# Let me check if N(W) can move DOWN past N(c)
# First, let me try moving DOWN once
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
